question: What three principles are written on the opening lecture slide, and how does the alpha-beta algorithm exemplify each of these principles as described during the lecture?
answer: The opening lecture slide shows three principles: DEAD HOLE PRINCIPLE, MARSHALL ARTS PRINCIPLE, and ANYTIME ALGORITHMS. Alpha-beta exemplifies the dead horse principle by not evaluating branches that cannot affect the outcome — it cuts off whole portions of the search tree where computation would be wasted. It exemplifies the martial arts principle by using the exponential growth characteristic of game trees against itself — because of the exponential blowup, computing at every level costs approximately the same as computing at just one level, enabling progressive deepening. Finally, progressive deepening itself is a prime example of an anytime algorithm because it always has an answer ready to deliver the moment time runs out.

ground_truth_spans.description: Opening slide shows DEAD HOLE PRINCIPLE, MARSHALL ARTS PRINCIPLE, ANYTIME ALGORITHMS
Lecture explains how alpha-beta and progressive deepening embody each of the three principles

question: What text is written on the blackboard in the frame caption showing the complete Deep Blue formula, listing all the components after the equals sign?
answer: The blackboard shows the following complete formula for Deep Blue written in chalk: DEEP BLUE = MINIMAX + Alpha + Beta + Computing + Opening Book + End Game + Uneven Tree Development. This formula lists all the components that make up Deep Blue's approach, starting from the basic minimax algorithm and adding alpha-beta pruning, computing power, an opening book of known chess moves, endgame databases, and finally uneven tree development as the most sophisticated additional element that contributed to beating Kasparov in 1997.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard caption shows DEEP BLUE = MINIMAX + Alpha + Beta + Computing + Opening Book + End Game + Uneven Tree Development

question: According to the lecturer, who besides Claude Shannon is credited with inventing the idea of using look-ahead and static evaluation to play chess, and in what unusual circumstances did this invention occur?
answer: According to the lecturer, Alan Turing is credited alongside Claude Shannon with inventing the idea of using look-ahead and static evaluation to play chess. Additionally, Donald Michie and Alan Turing also independently invented this approach. The unusual circumstances were that Turing invented this over lunch conversations — both Shannon and Turing spent a lot of lunchtime conversations talking with each other about how a computer might play chess in anticipation of a future when computers would exist, and Michie and Turing developed their version of the idea during lunchtime breaks from cracking the German codes during wartime.

ground_truth_spans.description: Claude Shannon, Alan Turing, and Donald Michie credited with inventing look-ahead evaluation; invented over lunch while cracking German codes

question: What visual label appears on the blackboard listing the four 'Ways to Play' chess, and which of those methods does the lecture describe as something layered on top of minimax rather than an alternative to it?
answer: The blackboard frame shows 'WAYS TO PLAY' as the heading with MINIMAX, α-β, PROGRESSIVE DEEPENING, and DEEP BLUE listed beneath it. The lecture explicitly states that alpha-beta is not an alternative to minimax but rather something layered on top of minimax to make it more efficient.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing blackboard with 'WAYS TO PLAY' heading and four methods listed
Lecture states alpha-beta is not an alternative to minimax but layered on top of it

question: The frame caption for the alpha-beta computer demo shows a menu with a 'Games' option selected and two numbers on each node. What does the lecture say those two numbers are called, and what happens to them as the algorithm proceeds?
answer: The frame caption shows a computer screen with the Minimax algorithm with alpha-beta pruning interface, with the 'Games' option highlighted in the menu. The lecture states that the two numbers on each node are called alpha and beta, and as the algorithm proceeds through the tree those numbers are going to shrink-wrap themselves around the situation.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing computer screen with Minimax alpha-beta pruning interface and Games menu selected
Lecture states two numbers are called alpha and beta and will shrink-wrap around the situation

question: A frame caption shows the blackboard listing 'ANALYSIS STRATEGY TACTICS' with 'IF-THEN RULES' and 'LOOK AHEAD & EVALUATE' as approaches. According to the lecture, which of these approaches works for checkers but cannot be made into a strong chess player?
answer: The frame caption shows the blackboard with 'ANALYSIS STRATEGY TACTICS,' 'IF-THEN RULES,' and 'LOOK AHEAD & EVALUATE' listed. The lecture states that someone has made a pretty good checkers-playing program using if-then rules — checking what moves are available, ranking them, and picking the highest — but nobody can make a very strong chess player that works that way.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing blackboard listing IF-THEN RULES and LOOK AHEAD & EVALUATE
Lecture states if-then rules work for checkers but cannot make a strong chess player

question: According to the lecture, what does Claude Shannon estimate the number of leaf nodes in the chess game tree to be, and why does this make the British Museum algorithm impossible?
answer: According to the lecture, Claude Shannon estimated there are about 10 to the 120th leaf nodes in the chess game tree. The British Museum algorithm would require doing 10 to the 120th static evaluations, and the lecture's calculation shows that even all atoms in the universe performing evaluations at nanosecond speeds since the Big Bang would still be 14 orders of magnitude short of that number.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture states Shannon estimated 10 to the 120th leaf nodes
Lecture calculates 14 orders of magnitude short, British Museum not feasible

question: According to the lecture, in what year did Deep Blue beat the world chess champion, and who had previously predicted that no computer would beat the world champion within 10 years?
answer: According to the lecture, Deep Blue beat the world champion in 1997. John McCarthy, noted as a founder of artificial intelligence, had previously predicted that no computer would beat the world champion within 10 years.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture states McCarthy predicted no computer would beat world champion in 10 years, and Deep Blue beat world champion in 1997

question: According to the lecture, what does the alpha-beta algorithm save besides static evaluations when it prunes branches of the game tree?
answer: According to the lecture, the alpha-beta algorithm saves not only static evaluations but also move generation — when a branch is cut off, those moves do not even need to be generated. The lecture states 'we save two ways, both on static evaluation and on move generation.'

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture states alpha-beta saves both static evaluation and move generation
